items.title: NFP +172K vs 85K consensus — December Fed hike fully priced, BNP calls 3 hikes
items.idea: May nonfarm payrolls came in at +172,000 — nearly double the 85K consensus. Traders now fully price a December Fed hike; BNP Paribas is calling for three. The 10Y cleared 4.5% and 30Y cleared 5%. April FOMC was already 8-4 to hold (most dissents since 1992). April PCE is at 3.8% YoY, highest since 2023, with core at 3.3%.
items.angle: This is the macro narrative fully flipping in one print. Rate cuts are dead. The debate is now how many hikes. That changes everything in the AI buildout thesis — 30Y above 5% is the new financing cost headwind.
items.tweetA: May NFP: 172K vs 85K expected. Double consensus. Market now pricing a December hike. BNP says 3 hikes. 10Y above 4.5%, 30Y above 5%. April FOMC was already 8-4. This is not a 'hot print' — this is a regime change.
items.tweetB: April PCE: 3.8% — highest since 2023. Core 3.3%. NFP doubled consensus. April FOMC: most dissents since 1992. Warsh is 15 days into the job. June 16-17 is his first real test. The market is pricing a hike before year-end. Are you positioned for a Fed that actually moves?

items.title: Macro regime shift: 'soft landing + rate cuts' → 'stagflation-lite + potential rate hikes'
items.idea: The NFP headline is misleading: job gains are from leisure & hospitality (World Cup prep), local government, and healthcare — 2 of 3 are short-lived. Meanwhile, retail and tech are cutting +66% YoY, with 40% of those cuts explicitly attributed to AI and automation displacement. High-wage jobs being replaced, low-wage/short-term jobs being added. The macro effect frames as shift to 'stagflation-lite.'
items.angle: The market is reacting to the headline number, not the composition. If 2/3 of the jobs added are cyclically transient, the Fed is tightening on false signal. Meanwhile AI displacement of high-wage jobs is a deflationary force in productivity but stagflationary in headline wages.
items.tweetA: The NFP was 172K but read the composition: World Cup hospitality prep, local gov hiring, demographic healthcare. 2/3 of it evaporates in 6 months. Meanwhile tech is cutting +66% YoY, 40% explicitly from AI. The Fed is about to hike on a jobs report that isn't real.
items.tweetB: Stagflation-lite is the new consensus. High-wage jobs replaced by AI. Low-wage/short-term jobs goosed by World Cup prep. The Fed has no idea which jobs are permanent. June 16-17 FOMC decides whether we hike into a fake labor market. $VIX $SPY

items.title: 2.6% drop from 'genuinely calm' regime — historically rare, only a handful of times in 75 years
items.idea: A 2.6% drop from a period of genuinely low realized volatility has occurred only a handful of times in 75 years. Paperalfa ran historical analysis back to the Nifty-Fifties era to identify named precedents. The move unravelled at 'record speed' — technical models caught a Nasdaq sell setup on Thursday, before the whole market violently adjusted. Bitcoin was already in a downmove after breaking $70K.
items.angle: When vol-regime transitions happen from the calmest of bases, the historical pattern is not mean reversion to calm — it's extended turbulence. You don't snap back instantly from a Minsky moment. The pattern analysis matters: what follows is rarely a straight recovery.
items.tweetA: A 2.6% drop from a dead-calm volatility regime has happened roughly a handful of times in 75 years. We just added to that list Friday. Technical models caught the Nasdaq sell setup Thursday — a day early. The question is not 'was Friday the bottom.' The question is what the next 30 days look like historically after this kind of snap.
items.tweetB: Friday felt violent. It was. Energy builds in quiet, then the system lets go — Minsky's actual definition. But the precedents back to the Nifty-Fifties suggest the post-snap pattern isn't V-shaped. It's messy. I'd want to see realized vol normalize before adding risk.

items.title: Iran: open-ended ceasefire + nuclear framework close — but IRGC hardliners ascendant, President resigned
items.idea: Trump announced an open-ended ceasefire extension Friday night while the US blockade continues until talks conclude 'one way or the other.' IAEA's Grossi signals US + Iran are close to a nuclear framework. CENTCOM shot down 4 Iranian drones near Hormuz and struck Iranian radar sites. Simultaneously: Iran President Pezeshkian reportedly resigned, with IRGC hardliners now ascendant. The war is ~3 months old — longest US Middle East conflict since Iraq invasion.
items.angle: The ceasefire + nuclear framework headline is the most constructive geopolitical signal of the cycle. But Pezeshkian's resignation with IRGC hardliners in control is the offsetting risk. A hardliner Iran makes any framework fragile. Oil priced the ceasefire relief — but the political risk in Tehran may not be over.
items.tweetA: Iran ceasefire extended. IAEA's Grossi says nuclear framework 'close.' Oil eased on the news. Also on Friday: Iran's president resigned, IRGC hardliners ascendant. The ceasefire the market is pricing may be signed by someone who won't be in power to honor it.
items.tweetB: 3 months of US-Iran conflict. Hormuz still blockaded — 20% of global oil and LNG flows. An open-ended ceasefire sounds good until you read that IRGC hardliners just took control of Tehran. Markets are pricing de-escalation. The political risk on the Iranian side is actually increasing. $USO $BNO

items.title: Goldman survey: 839 respondents overwhelmingly bearish on oil — the contrarian indicator is flashing
items.idea: Goldman surveyed 839 respondents and found overwhelming bearishness on oil. Wilson (HFIR) tracks this survey as a reliable contrarian indicator across 2016, 2018, 2020, and 2022 — each time wrong at the inflection. The survey was bullish in 2018 on 1.5mb/d of Iranian supply risk; today it's bearish on ~11mb/d of Hormuz blockade. 'Fastest visible onshore oil inventory declines continue.' Wilson is long USO, UCO, BNO.
items.angle: The brutal irony: the survey is maximally bearish while Hormuz — handling 20% of global oil and LNG — is effectively closed. In 2018, the survey was bullish with only 1.5mb/d of Iranian supply at risk. Today it's bearish with ~11mb/d off the table. If this survey is as contrarian as history suggests, the setup is historic.
items.tweetA: Goldman surveyed 839 oil market participants. They're overwhelmingly bearish. HFIR's track record on this survey: wrong at 2016, 2018, 2020, 2022 — every major inflection. In 2018 they were bullish on 1.5mb/d of Iranian risk. Now they're bearish on 11mb/d of Hormuz risk. Do the math. $USO $UCO
items.tweetB: Fastest visible onshore oil inventory declines in years. Hormuz handling 20% of global oil+LNG effectively closed. Goldman survey at maximum bearish. This is either the setup of the year or everyone has decided the ceasefire is real and priced. The answer arrives when Hormuz reopens or doesn't. $BNO $XOM

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING: Goldman survey bearishness vs historical oil market outcomes — shows contrarian indicator being wrong at every major inflection. The visual argument is devastating.

items.title: Oil math: 2018 survey bullish at 1.5mb/d Iranian risk; today bearish at 11mb/d Hormuz loss
items.idea: The quantitative case: Iranian sanctions in 2018 removed ~1.5 million b/d and survey participants were bullish (wrong). Today with ~11 million b/d off-line via Hormuz, the same participants are overwhelmingly bearish. The 7x larger disruption has produced the opposite survey sentiment. Wilson: 'Whoever Goldman is surveying is so clueless as to the point of blissful ignorance.' Long USO, UCO, BNO.
items.angle: The asymmetry is the signal. Historical supply disruptions of 1.5mb/d moved prices dramatically. 11mb/d is in another dimension. Either the market has correctly priced a very near-term resolution or this is the most mispriced commodity setup since COVID. The oil bear case requires Hormuz to reopen quickly and durably.
items.tweetA: Oil math that doesn't add up: 2018 Iranian sanctions = 1.5mb/d off. Survey: bullish. Today: ~11mb/d off via Hormuz. Survey: maximally bearish. The scale of the disruption is 7x larger and sentiment is the mirror image. Either the market is smarter than history or it's making the same mistake in reverse.
items.tweetB: Onshore oil inventories declining at the fastest pace in years. ~11mb/d blocked at Hormuz. Open-ended ceasefire but IRGC hardliners just took control in Tehran. The ceasefire could hold. The inventories can't hold forever. At some point price has to clear the math.

items.title: Gold two-phase selloff: first $60 was rational repricing — everything below $4400 was market mechanics
items.idea: NFP triggered a rational $60 drop (stronger growth, higher yields, hawkish Fed). But once gold broke the 200-DMA at $4,428 and the $4,400 psychological level, market structure took over: stop losses, momentum systems, retail long liquidation, dealer gamma hedging — all amplifying the slide. The author calls this 'two completely different markets.' By the time gold reached its lows, the market was trading liquidity, not economics.
items.angle: The cleanest framework for the gold selloff: the first move was trade-able and rational. The second was structural. The recovery timeline depends on whether structural seller exhaustion has occurred or whether gamma/positioning can extend the move. The author thinks the structural bull is intact.
items.tweetA: Gold's Friday selloff was actually two markets. First $60: textbook — higher yields, stronger growth, hawkish Fed. Everything after $4400: avalanche. Stop losses. Gamma hedging. Systematic selling. Retail liquidation. By the lows, the market wasn't trading payrolls. It was searching for liquidity. Two different signals, one chart.
items.tweetB: The same session that saw gold break $4400 also saw semis sell a trillion dollars. AI momentum crushed. Crypto capitulate. This is the tell: gold wasn't sold because people stopped believing in gold. Gold was sold because it was the one thing that still had a profit to take. Structural bull intact. Short-term — it's positioning.

charts.desc: Implied chart of gold breakdown through 200-DMA and $4400 level — two-phase structure visible

items.title: Gold sold for liquidity not loss of faith — open interest falling for months pre-NFP; structural buyer absence
items.idea: Open interest had been falling for months before the payroll shock. ETF flows had softened. The market was already running out of new buyers before the avalanche. Simultaneously: same session pressure across semiconductors, momentum trades, and AI complex suggests gold was monetized to cover losses elsewhere — not sold on conviction. Options gamma: as gold broke levels, dealer hedging amplified the move in a self-reinforcing feedback loop.
items.angle: The evidence of structural weakness pre-NFP reframes the selloff: this was a fragile market that the payroll print detonated. The recovery timeline depends on whether CB accumulation is enough to rebuild the base.
items.tweetA: Gold's open interest was already falling for months before Friday. ETF flows had softened. The mountain was losing hikers before the avalanche. NFP didn't cause the weakness — it revealed it. And then when gold broke, everyone who could still sell at a profit did. That's not a bearish thesis. That's a washout.
items.tweetB: Central banks keep buying gold. ETF investors keep harvesting profits. One group is trading the next payroll print. The other is positioning for the next decade. That tension is unresolved. The author thinks gold has a credible path to $8,000. The structural case didn't change Friday. The calendar did.

items.title: Author adjusting upside participation zone from $4,850 to $4,725; $8,000 target unchanged; downside hedge at $5,000
items.idea: The Dark Side of the Boom author discloses 2026 physical market purchases (in Thai Bars), holds physical from 2022 which remain 'comfortably hedged.' Downside hedge anchored around $5,000. As gold moves lower, those hedges activate sooner, increasing upside participation. Already adjusting upside participation zone from $4,850 toward $4,725. Long-term $8,000 target unchanged.
items.angle: Practitioner showing their actual hand: long physical since 2022, hedged around $5K, moving upside zone lower as price falls. The $8K target combined with systematic hedge adjustment is a meaningful conviction signal — not just commentary.
items.tweetA: The Dark Side of the Boom is long gold physical since 2022, hedged around $5K. As gold falls, the hedge structure activates sooner, increasing upside participation. They've already moved their upside zone from $4,850 to $4,725. This is the structural gold bull's actual positioning — not a tweet.
items.tweetB: I keep coming back to the gap: ETF investors selling gold profits, central banks accumulating at volume. When those two groups finally align — both buying — the move is going to be violent. Until then, every selloff is being absorbed by the group with a 10-year horizon. $GLD $PHYS

items.title: SpaceX/xAI IPO 'Tom Sawyer' thesis: Google pays $920M/month, Anthropic $1.25B/month for Colossus compute
items.idea: Elon Musk secured two companies to 'help paint his fence' ahead of the SpaceX/xAI IPO on June 12 ($1.77T valuation): Anthropic pays $1.25B/month for 220,000 Nvidia chips from Colossus through 2029; Google pays $920M/month for 110,000 Nvidia chips from Oct 2026 to June 2029. Both cancelable with 90 days notice. Google can cancel in October if chips aren't delivered. Combined: $2.17B/month of committed but terminable revenue.
items.angle: This is the IPO infrastructure play of the year. Musk turned excess xAI compute into $26B/year of contract revenue from AI rivals. The question for IPO investors: are these contracts durable or bridge arrangements that both parties cancel once their own capacity is built? The cancelability clause is the critical variable the IPO marketing glosses over.
items.tweetA: SpaceX IPO this week. Pre-read: Anthropic pays $1.25B/month for Colossus compute. Google pays $920M/month. Both cancelable with 90 days notice. Elon got his competitors to pay him to use his fence while he sells tickets to watch. Tom Sawyer is not a compliment — it's the valuation risk.
items.tweetB: Google has a right to cancel the SpaceX deal in October if SpaceX doesn't deliver the promised chips. Anthropic's deal runs 'through 2029.' Both have 90-day exit clauses. The $2.17B/month in contract revenue that anchors the $1.77T IPO valuation could be gone in a quarter. That's the risk no roadshow deck will tell you.

items.title: SpaceX IPO at 50x+ revenue: $75B raise is 3x the largest US IPO ever, $30B+ from individuals
items.idea: SpaceX is priced at over 50x revenue, vs Tesla at 15x and the S&P 100/500 at 3.6x. The $75B+ raise is 3x the largest US IPO in history. Over $30B comes from individual investors. Observers value the core business under $780B; over $1T of valuation is aspirational (AI space data centers, Terafab chip factories, Starlink expansion). Happening into a Nasdaq -4% week.
items.angle: Three overlapping bubbles in one IPO: Starlink (real revenue), AI compute (cancelable contracts), and space data centers ($1T+ aspirational). The individual investor allocation is the most striking: retail absorbing $30B+ of the largest IPO in US history in a risk-off tape.
items.tweetA: SpaceX IPO this week: 50x revenue. S&P 500 trades at 3.6x. Toyota at 0.75x. $75B raise — 3x the largest US IPO ever. $30B+ from individual investors. Into a tape where Nasdaq just posted its worst week since 2025. The supply/demand setup is the most interesting thing in markets next week.
items.tweetB: $780B is what the core SpaceX/Starlink business is worth by most observers. The IPO values it at $1.77T. The extra trillion is AI space data centers, Terafab chip factories, and plans to run Google's tech in orbit. Retail investors are buying the trillion, not the $780B. That's the bet.

items.title: Google Project Suncatcher: orbital data centers by 2027 with Planet Labs — space compute race is real
items.idea: Google has plans to launch orbital data centers by 2027 as part of 'Project Suncatcher,' working with Planet Labs. This is in parallel to renting SpaceX ground compute. Musk at JPMorgan said SpaceX's orbital centers would run 'chips from multiple companies including Google's proprietary tech.' SpaceX also bought $131M in excess Cybertrucks from Tesla and forced banking underwriters to commit millions to Grok use ahead of IPO.
items.angle: Two independent confirmations that orbital compute is no longer fantasy: Google's own 2027 target + SpaceX's roadmap. Planet Labs is an obscure entry in this story — worth watching as an enabler. The related-party aspects show the IPO pressure tactics throughout the Musk ecosystem.
items.tweetA: Buried in the SpaceX IPO story: Google has its own orbital data center program — Project Suncatcher, targeting 2027, using Planet Labs satellites. So Google is simultaneously renting SpaceX ground compute AND building its own space compute. The orbital data center race involves more players than Musk. $GOOG $PL
items.tweetB: SpaceX forced its IPO underwriters to commit millions to Grok use. Also bought $131M of Cybertrucks from Tesla. Also got Google and Anthropic to pre-pay for compute. The entire Musk ecosystem organized around one event: June 12 IPO. Related-party capitalism at its peak.

items.title: AI companies' revenues growing 5x faster than economy — Ramp firm-level data shows real divergence
items.idea: According to Ramp (fintech with firm-level spending data across the US), companies spending most on AI have seen revenue grow five times faster than the economy. Non-spenders track the economy closely. This echoes Bessen et al. 2020 AEA paper: Dutch firms that automated grew sales 2% faster, but automators were already more productive — selection effect matters.
items.angle: The Ramp data is genuinely interesting because it's firm-level, not sectoral. The Bessen parallel is the right counterpoint: automation tends to go to the already-productive, meaning we're measuring capability amplification, not broad economic lift. The divergence will widen, but it won't be universal.
items.tweetA: Ramp data: companies spending most on AI have revenue growing 5x faster than the economy. Non-AI-spenders: tracking GDP. This is the actual divergence showing up in real firm-level data. But Bessen's 2020 work is the counterpoint: automators were already the better operators. AI is amplifying the gap, not creating it.
items.tweetB: The AI productivity story has selection bias baked in: the companies buying the most AI were already the most productive. Ramp shows 5x revenue growth for AI-heavy firms vs GDP for the rest. But the 5x is probably not from AI alone — it's from being the kind of company that moves first. The rest will follow. Eventually.

charts.desc: Likely revenue divergence chart referenced — worth opening article

items.title: Anthropic: developer output 8x higher this quarter vs pre-Claude 4; 'Mythos' model shows step-change — available to NSA
items.idea: Anthropic released data: code contributed per developer is 8x higher in the current quarter vs average through end-2024. Meaningful uptick from 2025 as Claude 4 and Claude Code rolled out. A chart of Claude Code task completion by difficulty class shows a 'step change' for Mythos — Anthropic's latest model available only internally and to select organizations including the NSA. Mythos is not publicly released.
items.angle: The 8x developer output metric is the most concrete AI productivity figure from a major lab. NSA getting early Mythos access suggests government/defense is ahead of the public on capability curve. The 'step change' claim for an unreleased model is worth tracking.
items.tweetA: Anthropic: developer code output is 8x higher this quarter vs pre-Claude 4 average. They released the chart. Then buried in the footnotes: their newest model, Mythos, shows a 'step change' in capability. It's only available internally and to select orgs including the NSA. The public will see it later. The NSA sees it now.
items.tweetB: Lines of code per developer 8x — that's the Anthropic number. Whether lines of code is the right metric is debatable. But the trajectory from 2025 onward is unambiguous. Claude Code is changing how software gets built. The Mythos capability jump is the signal to watch for what comes next.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING: (1) Developer code output 8x growth — step-change from 2025 with Claude 4 rollout. (2) Claude Code task completion by difficulty class showing Mythos step-change. Both referenced explicitly.

items.title: Anthropic calls for option to 'slow or pause frontier AI'; Azeem skeptical — 'it's strategy not recursion, attenuated by chips/capital/power'
items.idea: Anthropic published data on recursive self-improvement risks and suggested 'it would be good for the world to have the option to slow or temporarily pause frontier AI development.' Azeem Azhar is skeptical for two reasons: (1) what's happening is 'increasingly abstract layers of automation accelerating product roadmaps of human-directed companies — not out-of-control machines' and (2) 'commercial realities act as a natural attenuator: securing capital, chips, and power will prove less tractable than pouring out reams of Python code.'
items.angle: Not doom vs no-doom, but 'is recursive self-improvement the right frame for what's actually happening?' The chips/power/capital constraint as natural speed limit is the pragmatic counterargument. Anthropic calling for a pause while being the company building the thing they want to pause is the obvious irony.
items.tweetA: Anthropic: we should have the option to pause frontier AI. Also Anthropic: our developer output is 8x higher. Also Anthropic: we're paying SpaceX $1.25B/month for more compute. The call to pause and the race to build are being done by the same company. That tension is the actual story.
items.tweetB: Azeem Azhar's take on 'recursive self-improvement' risk: 'We are dealing with increasingly abstract layers of automation accelerating human-directed product roadmaps — not runaway machines.' The natural speed limit is not safety regulation. It's capital, chips, and power. That's a more honest constraint.

items.title: AI fueling surge in new business formation — LLMs as 'mediocre but available' CFO/lawyer/marketer
items.idea: Torsten Slok data cited in Exponential View: AI is fueling a measurable surge in new business formation. Mechanism: 'a mediocre ChatGPT lawyer, finance director or marketing manager is, after all, better than none.' Technology reduces costs of certain cognitive activities; so more of them happen. Result: cheaper company launches, more entrepreneurship at the margin.
items.angle: Not the productivity gains at large firms, but the enabling effect at the smallest unit — the solo founder or tiny team. More companies change the competitive landscape over 5-10 years, create more venture opportunity, may partially explain the Ramp revenue divergence.
items.tweetA: The AI story everyone misses: new business formation is surging. Not just productivity at big firms. The marginal entrepreneur now has access to a mediocre-but-functional lawyer, CFO, and marketer via LLM. Torsten Slok is tracking it in the data. This is the long-run economic effect that matters most.
items.tweetB: A mediocre AI CFO is better than no CFO. That sentence explains why startup formation is accelerating. The fixed costs of building a company just got structurally lower. In 5 years that shows up as more competition in every market. In 10 years it shows up in productivity statistics. Right now it shows up in Slok's formation data.

items.title: BTC at $59,654 — lowest since pre-2024 election; crypto -48% from $4.2T peak; Fear & Greed at 12
items.idea: Bitcoin closed Friday at $59,654 (-6.12%), lowest since before the 2024 US election. ETH fell to $1,550 (-12.7%), SOL to $62.24, BNB $561, XRP $1.08. Total crypto market cap ~$2.18T, down 48% from $4.2T 2024 peak. Crypto Fear & Greed Index at 12 — extreme fear. BTC fell ~$17K in just over 24h mid-week. Fundstrat H1 bear targets for BTC ($60-65K), ETH ($1,800-2,000), SOL ($50-75) all hit or exceeded.
items.angle: Institutional bear case fully realized. ETF outflow streaks ended Thursday. Fear & Greed at 12. BTC at the 2024 election level. This is what exhaustion looks like on paper. The macro overhang (June 16-17 FOMC potential hike) is the only reason not to call this a bottom.
items.tweetA: BTC: $59,654. ETH: $1,550. Crypto market cap: -48% from peak. Fear & Greed: 12. Fundstrat H1 bear targets: hit. BTC/ETH ETF outflows: ended Thursday after 13 and 17 days. This is what technical exhaustion looks like in every metric simultaneously. The question is not whether it looks like a bottom. It's whether the Fed hikes on June 17.
items.tweetB: BTC is at the same level as the day after the 2024 US election. Everything since — the euphoria, the $100K+ run, the peak — has been erased. Fear & Greed at 12. The crypto bear case is fully priced. The macro bear case is not. That's the tension going into FOMC.

items.title: BTC + ETH spot ETF outflow streaks ended Thursday — 13 days/$4.4B and 17 days respectively
items.idea: The BTC spot ETF outflow streak ended Thursday after 13 consecutive days and $4.4B of outflows. The ETH spot ETF outflow streak ended Thursday after 17 consecutive days. First sign that the demand vacuum driving the entire decline is stabilizing. Friday's BTC recovery attempt to $62K after $59K capitulation adds to the signal.
items.angle: ETF flow data is the cleanest institutional demand signal in crypto. After 13 and 17 consecutive days of outflows respectively, both stopping on the same day is notable. Combined with Fear & Greed at 12 and bear targets hit, this is a multi-factor convergence. Still needs FOMC to clear.
items.tweetA: BTC ETF outflows: 13 straight days, $4.4B gone. Then Thursday: stopped. ETH ETF outflows: 17 straight days. Then Thursday: stopped. Both ending on the same day while Fear & Greed hits 12 and Fundstrat targets are hit. I've seen worse setups for a bounce. The variable is June 17. $BTC $ETH
items.tweetB: The demand vacuum that drove the entire crypto drawdown may be closing. BTC and ETH spot ETF outflow streaks both ended Thursday after 13 and 17 days. Not a recovery — an end to the hemorrhage. If the FOMC is a hawkish hold rather than a hike, that's the signal to add.

items.title: ZCash shielded pool bug: unlimited ZEC minting possible — exploit status unknown
items.idea: A bug was discovered in ZCash (ZEC) that allows unlimited ZEC minting within the shielded pool. Whether the bug has been exploited is currently unknown. This comes as ZEC was already down in the broader selloff. The shielded pool is ZCash's core privacy feature — a bug there is fundamental to the protocol's integrity.
items.angle: This is potentially existential for ZEC. The shielded pool is not a peripheral feature — it IS ZCash. If unlimited minting has occurred and is undetected, the supply economics are broken. The fact that exploit status is unknown is the scariest part.
items.tweetA: ZCash has a bug that allows unlimited ZEC minting within the shielded pool. Whether it's been exploited: unknown. The shielded pool is not a feature of ZCash — it is ZCash. This is the kind of undisclosed exploit risk that precedes a catastrophic move. If you hold ZEC, this is the one news item that matters.
items.tweetB: Buried under the Bitcoin capitulation and gold selloff: ZCash disclosed a bug allowing unlimited minting in the shielded pool. Exploit status: unknown. This is why privacy protocols carry tail risk that transparent chains don't — you can't verify what you can't see. The market hasn't fully priced this yet.

items.title: Arthur Hayes HYPE pump-and-dump: publicly pushed $150+ targets, sold at $70 — team unlock added pressure
items.idea: HYPE (Hyperliquid) sold off sharply. Arthur Hayes had been publicly promoting HYPE with $150+ price targets, then sold his position at $70. A team unlock coincided with the selloff, adding to the pressure. The author frames this as Hayes 'pumping and dumping on his followers.'
items.angle: The crypto influencer accountability story that will define discourse for weeks. Hayes had built a following on iconoclastic macro calls and genuine research. Using that credibility to move a token then exit is the kind of event that permanently shrinks a community.
items.tweetA: Arthur Hayes was pushing HYPE with $150+ targets. He sold at $70. Team unlock hit the same week. HYPE is now in the wreckage. This is not market timing — this is the definition of a pump and dump. If you followed Hayes into HYPE at triple digits, now you know what that trade was.
items.tweetB: The most damaging part of the HYPE story isn't the price — it's the trust. Hayes had built a following on iconoclastic macro calls and genuine research. Using that credibility to move a token then exit is the kind of thing that permanently shrinks a community. The crypto influencer model just got stress-tested.

items.title: Tech job cuts +66% YoY — 40% explicitly from AI displacement; high-wage out, low-wage in
items.idea: Tech and retail sector job cuts are running +66% year-over-year, with 40% of cuts explicitly attributed to AI/automation displacement. The NFP headline captures the replacement jobs (World Cup hospitality, local gov, healthcare) but not the structural hollowing of the high-wage labor market.
items.angle: The market is reacting to the headline number, not the composition. AI is simultaneously causing the job cuts AND providing the narrative for rate hikes. The Fed is tightening on a labor market being hollowed from the inside by the same technology fueling the AI capex boom.
items.tweetA: Tech job cuts: +66% YoY. 40% of those explicitly from AI displacement. The NFP headline of 172K is misleading because the composition is: World Cup hospitality prep + local gov + healthcare. High-wage tech jobs are being structurally removed. The Fed doesn't see that in the headline. It only sees 172K.
items.tweetB: AI is simultaneously causing the job cuts (tech -66% YoY, 40% from automation) AND providing the narrative for rate hikes (headline NFP beat). The Fed is tightening on a labor market being hollowed by the same technology that's fueling the AI capex boom. The irony is brutal.

items.title: Samsung 45,000-worker strike Day 12 — HBM/DRAM chokepoint at peak AI demand
items.idea: Samsung's 45,000-worker strike is on Day 12 of 18. Samsung produces HBM (High Bandwidth Memory) — the critical chip used in AI accelerators. The strike represents a supply chokepoint at the exact moment AI demand for HBM is at its peak. Micron was also hammered in the chip selloff, even as HBM remains sold out through 2026.
items.angle: Flying under the radar because the broader semi selloff dominates headlines. But if this extends beyond Day 18 or escalates, it directly impacts AI training capacity globally. HBM is not substitutable in the short term.
items.tweetA: Samsung 45K-worker strike, Day 12 of 18. HBM — the memory inside every AI accelerator — is produced largely at Samsung. Micron HBM is sold out through 2026. If this strike extends, every AI training cluster in the world feels it. The semi selloff is noise. The HBM supply chain is the signal. $MU $NVDA
items.tweetB: The AI selloff this week focused on capex peaking and rate headwinds. The overlooked risk: Samsung's 45K workers have been on strike for 12 days and make the memory that AI runs on. HBM sold out through 2026. Samsung strike deepening into Q3 = supply constraint for the inference buildout. Watch this.

items.title: Broadcom AI guide miss cascaded into $1T semi wipeout — HSBC flags chip-price slide + AI spending slowdown
items.idea: Broadcom kept its $100B AI revenue target unchanged despite weak guide, triggering a cascade selloff wiping ~$1 trillion from markets on the week. Nasdaq fell -3.97% Friday, worst since 2025 tariff turmoil. HSBC flagged 'chip-price slide + AI spending slowdown' as biggest worries. AI capex peaking debate now mainstream, compounded by 30Y above 5%.
items.angle: Broadcom maintained the $100B target — supposed to be bullish — but market read it as 'that's all you've got.' The unchanged guide in a rising rate environment means the NPV of AI revenue is declining even if top-line stays flat.
items.tweetA: Broadcom kept the $100B AI guide. The market sold a trillion dollars of semis anyway. The read: $100B is the ceiling, not the floor. When the numbers stop going up and rates go up, the NPV of the AI buildout compresses. $AVGO $NVDA $AMD — the AI revenue story needs a new number, not the same one.
items.tweetB: HSBC: 'chip-price slide + AI spending slowdown' are the biggest risks. Broadcom's unchanged $100B target in a 5% 30Y world is the data point. At 5% cost of capital, the AI capex math needs 2x the revenue to justify the same investment. The $100B target was written when 30Y was 4%. It's stale.

items.title: Warsh Day 15 as Fed Chair — June 16-17 FOMC is Test #1: hike vs hawkish hold, 8-4 dissent precedent
items.idea: Kevin Warsh is 15 days into his tenure as Fed Chair. The June 16-17 FOMC is his first policy decision. Previous FOMC voted 8-4 to hold at 3.50-3.75% — the most dissents since 1992. Markets fully price a December hike; BNP calls for three. The binary on June 17: hawkish hold (relief rally) vs actual hike (another leg down in risk assets).
items.angle: Warsh's first meeting sets the tone for his entire tenure. He has been consistently hawkish in public commentary. With 8-4 dissent already in the previous meeting, he may have the votes for a hawkish move. The market treats June 17 as a binary.
items.tweetA: Kevin Warsh: Day 15 as Fed Chair. June 17 is his first policy decision. Previous FOMC was already 8-4 to hold — most dissents since 1992. Market pricing a December hike. BNP says three. Warsh has been publicly hawkish for years. His first meeting is not a rubber stamp. It's a statement.
items.tweetB: The June 17 FOMC is the most important central bank meeting since Volcker. New Fed Chair, most internal dissent in 34 years, jobs doubled expectations, PCE at 3.8%, markets fully pricing a hike. If Warsh holds and sounds dovish, this is the risk-on trigger. If he hikes or signals three — another leg down.

items.title: Hormuz still closed — 20% of global oil + LNG; Bill Pulte (FHFA) named acting intelligence chief
items.idea: The Strait of Hormuz, handling approximately 20% of global oil and LNG flows, remains effectively closed 3 months into the US-Iran conflict (since Feb 28). CENTCOM shot down 4 Iranian drones and struck Iranian radar sites Friday. Separately: Trump named FHFA's Bill Pulte as acting intelligence chief — an unusual appointment crossing financial regulatory and national security roles.
items.angle: The Hormuz closure is the most underappreciated macro variable: 20% of global energy transit is blocked for 3 months and oil is at $92-97. The ceasefire gives a reason for optimism but Pulte as acting intel chief is a bizarre institutional signal — the FHFA oversees Fannie/Freddie, not intelligence.
items.tweetA: Hormuz has been effectively closed for 3 months. ~20% of global oil and LNG flows. Oil is at $92-97. The market has priced a quick resolution since day 1. Three months later, IRGC hardliners are in control of Tehran and CENTCOM is still shooting down drones. The optimism is faith-based at this point.
items.tweetB: Trump named the FHFA director as acting intelligence chief. The FHFA is Bill Pulte's current job — he oversees Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, the housing finance regulator. Now running intelligence. In a week when Iran is the defining geopolitical story. This is the institutional signal that's hardest to interpret.

items.title: JPMorgan + BofA + Citi shared tokenized network; DTCC chose Stellar XLM (+40%) for tokenized securities
items.idea: JPMorgan, Bank of America, and Citi will launch a shared tokenized network next year to compete with stablecoins — CLARITY Act stablecoin yield is the sticking point. DTCC chose Stellar (XLM) as its blockchain for tokenized securities — XLM surged 40%+ during the week. CFTC also cleared Coinbase global crypto perpetual futures via Deribit.
items.angle: Three simultaneous moves in financial market infrastructure: big bank tokenization, DTCC choosing a specific blockchain, CFTC clearing crypto perps. The plumbing layer of the crypto/TradFi convergence is becoming real. XLM's 40% move on the DTCC news is the template for how regulatory/infrastructure selection drives returns.
items.tweetA: DTCC chose Stellar (XLM) for tokenized securities. XLM: +40%. JPMorgan, BofA, and Citi announced a shared tokenized network for next year. CFTC cleared Coinbase global crypto perps via Deribit. Three infrastructure moves in one week while the market was focused on the crypto drawdown. The plumbing is being built in the panic. $XLM
items.tweetB: The CLARITY Act stablecoin bill is running out of time — JPMorgan says so. The sticking point: stablecoin yield. Big banks want tokenized deposits (they keep the yield). Stablecoin issuers pass yield to holders. This is the lobbying fight that decides whether stablecoins or bank tokens win the next decade of payments.

items.title: Strategy sold 32 BTC (first since 2022) to fund dividends; holds 818K BTC at $75,700 avg cost — $13B+ underwater
items.idea: Strategy (formerly MicroStrategy) sold 32 BTC (~$2.5M) — its first Bitcoin sale since 2022 — to fund dividend obligations. Saylor publicly framed the BTC price drop as 'AI rotation, not BTC being broken.' Strategy's total holding: ~818,000 BTC at an average cost basis of $75,700 — deeply underwater at current $59,654. ONDO lost 17% after founder Nathan Allman died unexpectedly.
items.angle: Selling 32 BTC to fund dividends when you hold 818,000 suggests the dividend obligation is not trivially fundable from operating cash flow. Saylor's 'AI rotation' framing is spin — the position is $13B+ underwater. This is the structural vulnerability of the MSTR levered BTC thesis.
items.tweetA: Strategy sold 32 BTC to pay dividends. First sale since 2022. They hold 818,000 BTC at a $75,700 avg cost. BTC is at $59,654. They're $13B+ underwater. Saylor says this is 'AI rotation, not BTC being broken.' At some point the dividend math and the unrealized loss math intersect. That intersection is worth watching.
items.tweetB: MSTR holds 818K BTC at $75,700 cost basis. Current price: ~$60K. That's a $13B unrealized loss. And they're selling BTC — first sale in 4 years — to fund dividends. If you're looking for forced sellers in the next leg down, start here. The levered BTC thesis has a very specific weak point.

items.title: Better + Coinbase issued first Fannie Mae-backed crypto mortgage — Fannie now accepting crypto as collateral
items.idea: Better (mortgage lender) and Coinbase issued the first-ever Fannie Mae-backed mortgage using crypto as collateral. Fannie Mae has formally decided to accept crypto assets as qualifying collateral for conforming mortgages. This is a regulatory/institutional milestone: the government-sponsored enterprise backing $7T+ in US mortgage debt validated crypto as collateral.
items.angle: Quietly one of the most significant institutional crypto milestones — not a bank custody approval or ETF, but the actual mortgage infrastructure accepting crypto as collateral. The implication: crypto wealth can now fund housing without selling.
items.tweetA: Fannie Mae now accepts crypto as mortgage collateral. Better + Coinbase issued the first one. This is not a small milestone — Fannie backs $7T+ in US mortgages. Crypto just became qualifying collateral in the government housing finance system. That's a more durable institutional signal than an ETF approval.
items.tweetB: The first Fannie Mae-backed crypto mortgage was issued this week. Nobody is talking about it because BTC is at $60K. When the cycle turns and BTC is back at $80K+, the fact that crypto can now collateralize conforming mortgages will matter a lot. File this under 'structural wins during the drawdown.'

items.title: Polymarket: ~52% probability of Fed hike before year-end; April PCE 3.8% YoY — stagflation setup completing
items.idea: Polymarket now shows ~52% probability of a Fed rate increase before year-end. Average hourly earnings ticked DOWN to 3.4% YoY (wage relief) while PCE reaccelerates to 3.8% YoY — highest since 2023, core at 3.3%. The combination of declining real wages, rising goods inflation, and hot headline employment is the textbook stagflation setup.
items.angle: Wage deceleration (3.4%) alongside PCE reacceleration (3.8%) is the nightmare combination: workers getting less while prices go up more. The Fed is being asked to tighten into this — which helps neither problem. Stagflation breaks all the macro models.
items.tweetA: Wage growth: 3.4% YoY and falling. PCE inflation: 3.8% YoY and rising. Real wages: negative. Fed: market pricing a hike. This is the textbook stagflation setup. The Fed can't fix this with rate hikes — supply constraints don't respond to higher rates. But it's hiking anyway.
items.tweetB: Polymarket: 52% chance of a Fed rate hike before year-end. That's a coin flip — 6 months ago it was near zero. The regime that was 'higher for longer but no hikes' is becoming 'higher for longer AND hikes.' If that sticks, every long-duration asset needs to be repriced again.

items.title: Capital flowing selectively to AI infrastructure, privacy, and sports markets in crypto despite broad drawdown
items.idea: Despite the broad crypto selloff, capital is flowing selectively to: (1) AI infrastructure projects (compute coordination, inference markets), (2) privacy protocols (despite ZCash bug concern), (3) sports prediction markets and fan tokens (World Cup proximity driving activity). Relative strength within the carnage — sectors with narrative catalysts holding.
items.angle: In every deep selloff, the winners of the next cycle reveal themselves by holding. The AI-crypto intersection is the most interesting: on-chain AI compute markets are the natural next evolution as off-chain AI costs compress. Sports markets with World Cup proximity have a hard date-specific catalyst.
items.tweetA: Everything is down. Not everything equally. In the crypto selloff, capital is selectively flowing to: AI compute infrastructure projects, privacy protocols, and World Cup sports markets. In every cycle's worst weeks, the next cycle's winners reveal themselves by their relative strength. Worth watching what's holding.
items.tweetB: The AI-crypto intersection: on-chain inference markets, compute coordination protocols, AI agent token frameworks. These are holding relative to the broad selloff. If AI is the macro theme and crypto is looking for a narrative, AI-native crypto projects are the intersection trade. The draw is still away but positioning is happening now.
